title: Chalmers unveils biggest attack on wealth since federation
full_text:
The 2026 Chalmers budget is the highest risk budget in modern history and arguably the biggest locally generated attack on the wealth of the nation since federation. It is the left wing equivalent of Trump’s US right wing revolution.
Chalmers might theoretically argue that his wealth destruction, and the consequent lower community spending and higher unemployment, may force the Reserve Bank to lower interest rates
The wealth attack was made possible because the Opposition is in chaos which means the government believes it has 5.5 years of government so it has a unique opportunity to advance an extreme left wing agenda
Here are just a few the carefully targeted blows to advance that agenda:
Ending the 25 year housing boom particularly on houses worth above $1.5 /2m.and below $10bn (in some areas of Australia housing wealth destruction will start at slightly different levels).
On their own the end of negative gearing and the capital gains tax might not have triggered an end to the housing boom but, as I will explain below, Chalmers is diving those nails into an already depressed housing sector.
For a quarter of a century banks, insurance companies and the real estate industry have generated huge profits from the boom.
Accordingly, the housing fall will impact the profits of banks via lower lending amounts and a rise in problem loans---particularly on lending in the last three years of the boom. . Lower dwelling prices will also impact insurance companies, the real estate industry plus state and local governments.
All those enterprises impacted will be forced into using AI and other means to slash costs creating great nervousness among those who took out big mortgages and have vulnerable highly paid jobs.
One of the highest capital gains taxes in the world is set to savage not just investment housing but a wide range assets led by the share market. Naturally, superannuation funds and later trusts will not escape the Chambers’ wealth destruction, particularly via parts of the share market and the value of businesses. .
Chalmers has created “islands” in shares, housing and other assets where wealth is grandfathered from the capital gains tax and negative gearing but the capital gains tax/ negative gearing removal that will be later levied on potential buyers means the asset is worth significant less to the buyer than to the seller.
Among the first impacted by the grandfathered islands’ will be those trying to sell apartments off the plan to investors. Eventually those apartments will be bought by investors who are subject of the full weight of capital gains taxing and the absence of negative gearing.
Young people starting a business are initially protected but the potential buyers of any successful business they develop will discount the value. Young people will quickly wake up that Chalmers set them a trap.
In his speech Chambers highlights that since 1990 dwelling prices gave risen 400 per cent or twice the rate of average income.
At the start of the boom most bank loans were designed to be paid off in 20 years. As the loan levels on the same dwellings got bigger the repayment dates were increased to as high as 35 years.
But now many dwellings that the banks financed at prices between about $2m and $10m in NSW and Victoria have already fallen 20 per cent (sometimes the discount reaches 25 per cent) wiping out chunks of equity for recent buyers. equity. (Other states have performed better).
Why had the market cracked before the Chambers blows?
For much of those 25 years interest rates were contained but they are now rising, and the latest big spending budget increases the risk of greater interest rate rises---unless the downturn created by the wealth destruction forces the Reserve Bank to lower rates.
In the boom at weekend barbecues people swapped dwelling profit stories. Now with equity ratios in decline it is safer talk about the football or even One Nation. The housing silence is infectious.
For most of the boom enormous amounts of Asian capital flooded into Australia buying houses and apartments. The Asian money flood multiplied the local boom forces.
But now the Asian money tap has been turned off (they cant get the money out) and the owners of many of those properties want to sell. They are finding it very difficult
Initially state governments taxed dwellings and land moderately but in the last decade stamp duty, GST, land taxes, foreign buyer taxes have become a honey pot. States used the boom the fund huge expenditures, but they went too far. These revenue sources will now become unsustainable as the price of dwellings falls.
Sky News Business Editor Ross Greenwood says changes to Capital Gains Tax in Labor’s 2026 budget will force entrepreneurs to move to countries like Singapore with “zero CGT”.
In addition a public service elite deliberately restrained dwelling development and increased the costs. The budgets sets out moves to stop this which is good news.
The housing down turn will also impact business lending. Most small and business loans are secured on a dwelling. The fall in dwelling prices will limit the amount many banks will offer smaller enterprises.
Meanwhile, the best tax advantage in dwellings remains the residential house. Australians have saved this way on a major scale and Australian houses are the largest in the world. But fall will impact f this way of savings
What will cushion the fall is that the cost of new dwellings is way above the market value of existing dwellings. But despite government incentives the difference will limit the rate of new construction.
The Chalmers’ capital gains tax will raise nothing like what the treasurer expects and will land blows on the up-and-coming generation trying to sell successful businesses they develop.
My guess is that the current capital gains tax will be amended before the next election but only if the Liberals
and /or One Nation become a serious threat to the government in 2028.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.

summary: The 2026 Chalmers budget introduces significant changes to capital gains tax and negative gearing, impacting housing prices and wealth distribution in Australia.
author: Robert Gottliebsen
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: The budget is described as the largest attack on national wealth since federation, targeting high-value properties and investment assets.
Changes to capital gains tax and negative gearing are expected to lower housing prices and affect bank profits, insurance companies, and local governments.
The budget may force entrepreneurs to relocate to countries with more favourable tax regimes, impacting business growth and investment.
relevance: These developments could significantly affect investment strategies and financial planning for actuaries and financial services professionals.
